risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Subjects were randomly assigned to either the TM or health education (HE) arms using a stratified block design. ... Random allocation was performed by the study biostatistician who concealed the allocation schedule and conveyed the assignments to the study coordinator." Comment: This describes a stratified block randomization process with allocation performed by a biostatistician, which is consistent with a truly random sequence generation method and is sufficient for Low risk under the stated permissive criteria.

Quote: "Random allocation was performed by the study biostatistician who concealed the allocation schedule and conveyed the assignments to the study coordinator." Comment: This explicitly states that the allocation schedule was concealed from the recruiter/enrolling personnel, which is sufficient to judge low risk for allocation concealment because it prevents foreknowledge of the next assignment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Since double blinding in behavioral trials is generally not feasible, this was a single-blinded trial." Comment: Participants and intervention personnel were not blinded to treatment allocation; although investigators/data staff were blinded, the study explicitly states single-blinding only. Because the trial included subjective outcomes (e.g., blood pressure-related behaviors, psychosocial distress, adherence, and quality-of-life-type measures) that could plausibly be influenced by knowing assignment, this domain is at high risk overall.

Quote: "Investigators, data collectors and data management staff were blinded to group assignment. Since double blinding in behavioral trials is generally not feasible, this was a single-blinded trial." Also: "All clinical end points were adjudicated by a blinded, independent reviewer who applied standardized and validated criteria." Comment: Outcome assessment for the main clinical endpoints was done by a blinded independent reviewer, and these endpoints (mortality, MI, stroke, revascularization, hospitalization) are objective and unlikely to be influenced by knowledge of allocation. Although several intermediate outcomes were self-reported or clinician-measured, the study explicitly states that data collectors were blinded and the reported outcome assessment for the clinical endpoints was blinded, supporting low risk for detection bias in the domain.

Quote: "The rate of nonparticipation in the treatment groups was 19/99 or 19% in the TM group and 10/102 or 10% in the HE group" and "At the beginning of phase 2, 143 subjects were re-enrolled in the second phase. Fifty-eight subjects from phase 1 did not participate in phase 2 because of death, attrition or lack of informed consent." Comment: Attrition was not trivial and was imbalanced across arms in phase 1 (19% vs 10%, a 9 percentage-point difference), and a substantial number of participants did not continue into phase 2. For outcomes measured over follow-up, the paper also reports incomplete availability of nonfatal event data for non-reenrolled subjects. Although the authors used ITT/mixed models and a sensitivity analysis for fatal events, the raw loss and incomplete follow-up data create a plausible risk of attrition bias.
